sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with right knee pain that began 5 days ago after water skiing, where he twisted his knee while landing a jump and experienced a sudden onset of pain and swelling.
He denies hearing or feeling anything pop at the time of injury.
He has been taking over-the-counter Aleve twice a day and applying a cold pack to the affected area, which has provided some relief but the pain persists.
Past medical history: The patient has had his tonsils removed.
He has been taking lisinopril 20mg daily for hypertension, which he reports has been effective with no side effects.
Review of systems: The patient denies numbness or tingling in his foot, and his pain is localized to the right knee.
Current medications: Lisinopril 20mg daily for hypertension.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is 120/77, which is within a normal range.
Physical exam: The patient has ecchymosis and edema on the right knee, with an effusion present.
Palpation of the right medial knee is painful.
The patient has decreased range of motion, negative varus and valgus test, negative Lachman sign, and palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse.
The lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a medial collateral ligament strain in the right knee.
Problem list: Medial collateral ligament strain right knee confirmed; hypertension; due for tetanus shot.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam 15mg once daily for pain and inflammation.
I ordered an X-ray of the right knee to rule out any broken bones.
I referred the patient to physical therapy to strengthen the muscles around the knee.
I will continue the patient on lisinopril 20mg daily for hypertension and ordered a lipid panel to check for any abnormalities.
I administered a tetanus shot for health maintenance.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me after the results of the X-ray and lipid panel are available, and will rest the knee for a while to avoid further injury.
